name: Drying and Preserving Foods Part One
recipeIngredient: 4 cups sliced apples (for fruit leather recipe)
2 lbs beef, cut into strips (for jerky)
1 bag fresh mixed nuts and seeds (for drying)
recipeInstructions:
1. Food drying is one of the oldest methods of preserving food for later use. It can either be an alternative to canning and freezing or compliment these methods.
2. Drying removes moisture from the food so that bacteria, yeasts and molds cannot grow and spoil it. It also slows down enzyme action but does not inactivate them.
3. The optimum temperature for drying is 140°F. Higher temperatures will cook the food instead of drying it out, leading to "case hardening" which can mold if left too long.
4. Sun-drying fruits like grapes when temperatures are at least 85°F and humidity below 60% works best. However, weather unpredictability makes this risky for perishable foods.
5. Vegetables with low sugar/acid content or meats should not be dried outdoors due to spoilage risks and high protein content making them susceptible to microbial growth.
6. For safe drying at home, use a food dehydrator set between 140-150°F, with low humidity and good air circulation.
7. Rehydrate dried foods by adding water before eating or cooking. The soaking liquid can be used in soups or dishes for added flavor.
8. Drying preserves the shape of the food when moisture is reintroduced and helps retain nutrients compared to cooking methods that use high heat or steam.
